unit.name: Serialization & Track-and-Trace System
unit.cfj: Assign, print, and verify unique serial identifiers on labels to meet regulatory serialization requirements

meta.method: Ulwick ODI (Importance × Satisfaction → Opportunity), needs scoring v2

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of saleable units cleared for release without a serial identifier
rows.imp_rat: Saleable units cleared for release without a serial fully fails the assign requirement at the last gate; severity is high because unserialized product shipped is a clear compliance breach.
rows.sat_rat: End-of-batch reconciliation plus release holds is a standard control that reliably blocks unserialized saleable units, with rare escapes from manual disposition errors. Batch reconciliation at disposition catches missing-serial units before release without materially delaying the run itself. Reconciliation reports are largely automated, so the QA reviewer needs modest skill to spot a missing identifier. Rework or destruction of unserialized units is costly, but the release-hold control prevents most from reaching sale.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of assigning the same serial to multiple units after processing resumes
rows.imp_rat: Assigning the same serial to multiple units after resume is a uniqueness failure at a known failure point; severe because restart duplicates undermine the whole run's integrity.
rows.sat_rat: Restart/recovery is a recognized weak spot where duplicate assignment after resume still occurs despite checkpoints. Resume logic executes quickly once recovery procedures start. Correct resume requires operator judgement about where the line stopped, a known weak point. Re-assigning serials after resume risks scrapping affected units.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of units carrying a serial already assigned to another unit
rows.imp_rat: Units carrying a duplicate serial is the physical manifestation of a uniqueness failure central to the CFJ; severe because such units cannot be reliably traced or authenticated.
rows.sat_rat: Unique-serial enforcement across units is a core, well-proven feature, though cross-site or cross-vendor pool merges still occasionally slip. Unit-level serial commissioning happens in line with printing, adding no meaningful delay. Automated pool management removes the operator's need to manually track which serials are consumed. Duplication protection is built into existing serialization licenses at little extra cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that changes to applicable serialization mandates are omitted from release compliance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Changed mandates omitted from release criteria means the job is measured against outdated rules; severe because product may ship non-compliant with current regulations.
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory tracking is largely manual and fragmented across markets, so omissions of mandate changes from release criteria are a recurring, poorly controlled risk. Incorporating mandate changes into release criteria is a periodic regulatory-tracking task, not a run-time delay. It depends heavily on scarce regulatory affairs expertise to interpret and translate evolving mandates into criteria. Maintaining regulatory intelligence is a staffing cost, but missing a change risks far larger noncompliance costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of saleable units whose printed serial identifier conflicts with the electronic serialization record at product release
rows.imp_rat: Printed serial conflicting with the electronic record at release breaks physical-to-digital correspondence central to serialization; severe as it fails regulatory reconciliation.
rows.sat_rat: Aggregation and commissioning records generally align printed identifiers with the electronic record, but edge-case mismatches at release still surface. Reconciliation between printed and electronic records happens at release with modest delay. Cross-checking print records against the serialization repository is semi-automated but still needs analyst attention. Discrepancy investigations at release consume QA resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of units passing the assignment point without a serial identifier
rows.imp_rat: Units passing the assignment point without a serial directly defeats the CFJ of assigning unique identifiers; severity is high because unserialized saleable product breaches regulatory serialization mandates and can trigger recalls.
rows.sat_rat: Mature vision-and-print interlocks that stop the line on a no-print event make units passing without a serial rare in deployed systems. Inline serialization printers assign codes at line speed without adding time per unit once configured. Automated assignment triggered by the line means operators need little skill to ensure each unit gets a serial. Missing-serial units become waste, but modern reject stations catch most before they consume packaging cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of labels assigned a serial already assigned to another label
rows.imp_rat: Duplicate serials on different labels violate the core uniqueness requirement of the job; consequences are severe as duplicate identifiers undermine track-and-trace and invite regulatory rejection.
rows.sat_rat: Mature Level-2/ software enforces unique allocation with database constraints, making within-system label duplicates rare where deployed. Serial assignment is near-instant, so time is not the pain here. Modern serialization systems auto-generate and check uniqueness so operators need little skill to avoid duplicates. Duplicate-avoidance logic adds negligible marginal cost once the system is licensed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of serial identifiers assigned to more than one saleable unit
rows.imp_rat: A serial assigned to more than one saleable unit is the textbook uniqueness defect central to the job; severe because it invalidates authentication for all affected units.
rows.sat_rat: Centralized serial-number generators with database uniqueness constraints make duplicate assignment to two units rare under standard practice. Serial number management systems allocate identifiers instantly, adding no delay. Uniqueness is enforced by the number-generation database, removing reliance on human tracking. Serial pool management is a core function of existing L4/L5 systems, so no added cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of labels assigned a serial for the wrong product, lot, package level, or market
rows.imp_rat: Serials for the wrong product/lot/level/market misassign identity central to serialization; severe because misidentified product can be recalled or rejected at the border.
rows.sat_rat: Job-based configuration links serials to product/lot/market, but misconfiguration at changeover remains a frequent real-world failure. Correct context binding happens at job setup and does not slow the run. Operators must correctly select product/lot/market at changeover, a step still prone to human error. Wrong-context serials trigger costly rework and scrap of labeled units.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recovered units whose printed serials differ from their electronic serialization records
rows.imp_rat: Recovered units whose printed serials differ from electronic records break physical-digital correspondence during recovery; severe because mismatched units fail reconciliation at release.
rows.sat_rat: Print-vs-record divergence after recovery is a known, imperfectly handled failure mode. Reconciling recovered units against records takes investigation time. Matching physical prints to electronic records after recovery needs skilled analysis. Mismatched recovered units may be scrapped, incurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of units assigned a serial identifier already assigned to another unit
rows.imp_rat: Assigning a serial already given to another unit is a direct uniqueness violation central to the job; severity is high due to regulatory non-conformance and traceability breakdown.
rows.sat_rat: Centralized serial-number generators with commissioned-status tracking make duplicate assignment very rare in practice. Serial number managers dispense unique codes instantly with no added cycle time. Uniqueness is enforced by software allocation, requiring no operator vigilance. Duplicate serials would force rework or scrapping, but automated dispensing prevents this cheaply.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a unit with a mismatched or unreadable code being cleared to advance
rows.imp_rat: A mismatched/unreadable unit cleared to advance is the direct escape of a verify failure; severe because defective product reaches market undetected.
rows.sat_rat: Verify-and-reject stations are a mature control, though reject-mechanism faults occasionally let unreadable units through. Reject decisions are made inline in real time. Automated pass/fail gating removes reliance on operator judgement to pull bad units. Preventing bad units from advancing avoids expensive market recalls.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of units released downstream without completed code verification during recovery
rows.imp_rat: Units released downstream without completed verification during recovery is a direct verify escape; severe because recovery conditions let unverified product reach market.
rows.sat_rat: Recovery procedures aim to block unverified release, but ad hoc restart handling makes this a recognized weak area. Recovery gating adds hold time before release. Recovery decisions rely on operator judgement about verification completeness. Releasing unverified units risks costly recalls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required serialization event data not submitted to designated trading partners or authorities
rows.imp_rat: Required event data not submitted to partners/authorities breaks the regulatory reporting obligation; severe because non-submission is a direct compliance violation.
rows.sat_rat: EPCIS-based reporting is standard, but connection failures, format mismatches, and rejected messages regularly leave some required events unsubmitted. Reporting to authorities/partners runs on scheduled batch submissions, so timeliness depends on interface reliability. Managing diverse partner and regulatory reporting formats requires specialized integration knowledge. Failed submissions can block sale or incur penalties, making gaps costly relative to the reporting infrastructure cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of duplicate serial identifiers assigned to different saleable units in a release lot
rows.imp_rat: Duplicate serials on different saleable units in a release lot is a direct failure of unique assignment; severity is high because it corrupts lot integrity and can force lot-level holds.
rows.sat_rat: Mature number-management engines enforce uniqueness within a lot almost flawlessly, making duplicates within a release lot a rare failure. Serialization systems assign identifiers in real time so timing is rarely the bottleneck for duplicate prevention. Standard L4/L5 serialization platforms auto-generate ranges without requiring operator judgement to avoid collisions. Duplicate-prevention logic is built into commissioned systems at no marginal per-lot cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of packaged units with failed identifier verification that proceed downstream
rows.imp_rat: Units with failed verification proceeding downstream defeats the 'verify' pillar of the CFJ; severity is high because unverified/bad codes reach market and threaten patient safety and compliance.
rows.sat_rat: Camera-based verification with automatic reject is a mature, widely deployed practice, though mechanical reject failures and manual overrides still let occasional bad units through. Inline vision/verification stations reject failed units in real time, so latency between failure and diversion is minimal on modern lines. Once configured, automated reject gates require little operator judgement to keep failed units from proceeding downstream. Reject-and-quarantine hardware is standard capital on serialization lines, so the incremental cost per run is modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serial identifiers assigned under the wrong product or lot context
rows.imp_rat: Serials assigned under wrong product/lot context misattributes identity central to serialization; severe because it corrupts lot genealogy and traceability.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-based setup reduces context errors but manual batch selection at startup still allows occasional wrong-context assignment. Correct product/lot context is set at batch setup with no per-unit time. Operator must select the right batch/product at setup, a known error-prone judgement point. Wrong-context assignment can invalidate a whole batch, a large cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of commissioned serials associated with multiple physical units
rows.imp_rat: Commissioned serials tied to multiple physical units directly violates uniqueness in the master record; severe because authentication becomes ambiguous.
rows.sat_rat: Inline commissioning generally binds one serial per unit, but jams and manual interventions can create multiply-associated serials. Commissioning happens inline without added delay. Automated one-serial-per-unit commissioning limits operator error. Multi-unit serials cause reconciliation cost and potential recalls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of rejected units reentering packaging without valid unique serial identifiers
rows.imp_rat: Rejected units reentering packaging without valid serials reintroduces unserialized/invalid product; severe because it silently defeats prior controls.
rows.sat_rat: Reject reintroduction is often a manual, procedure-driven step where lapses still occur despite status controls. Manual disposition of rejects and reintroduction checks add handling time. Operators must correctly follow segregation procedures, making outcome dependent on human discipline. Improper reintroduction risks duplicate or invalid serials entering commerce with high recall cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a test or prior-run value remains in the active production configuration
rows.imp_rat: Test/prior-run values left in the active config misidentifies real production output; severe because a single stale value can invalidate an entire batch's serials.
rows.sat_rat: Procedures exist but stale test/prior-run values slipping into production is a well-known, imperfectly controlled failure. Clearing test values is a quick pre-run check when procedures are followed. Detecting leftover test/prior data relies heavily on operator diligence at changeover. A test value in production can void a run, causing significant scrap cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required serialization data elements missing from records used for product release
rows.imp_rat: Required data elements missing from release records defeats the ability to demonstrate compliance; severe because incomplete release records can block shipment or fail audit.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic batch records enforce many required fields, but incomplete or unmapped serialization data elements still surface at release under current practice. Completeness checks at release run against the batch record without significant delay. Determining which data elements are required demands regulatory and QA expertise per market. Missing elements delay release and rework records, a moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the serial sequence resuming from the wrong point after interruption
rows.imp_rat: Serial sequence resuming from the wrong point after interruption causes duplicates or gaps; severe because restart errors can duplicate identifiers across many units.
rows.sat_rat: Sequence resume after interruption remains a recognized weak spot where the wrong restart point still occurs. Resuming after interruption often needs manual verification that costs time. Operator must correctly identify the resume point, an error-prone judgement. Wrong resume points create duplicates or gaps requiring costly rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of packages bearing an identifier format not accepted in the destination market
rows.imp_rat: Identifier format not accepted in the destination market defeats the market's serialization requirement; severe because shipments can be blocked at customs or recalled.
rows.sat_rat: Market format templates are maintained in serialization systems, but the breadth of evolving country requirements leaves recurring risk of an unaccepted format. Format selection is set at configuration and does not delay the run once correct. Choosing the market-accepted identifier format requires regulatory and technical knowledge per destination. Wrong-format product can be rejected at border or by customers, an expensive rework or loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serial identifiers associated with the wrong physical unit
rows.imp_rat: Serials associated with the wrong physical unit breaks the identifier-to-unit link at the heart of track-and-trace; severe because traceability is inverted.
rows.sat_rat: Camera-to-print synchronization is generally reliable but reject-handling and buffer shifts can still tie a serial to the wrong physical unit. Inline association happens at print time without adding time. Timing between print and camera must be tuned, so some technical setup skill matters. Misassociation causes rework and investigation cost when physical and record differ.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that configured allocations create duplicate serial identifiers within or across production runs
rows.imp_rat: Allocation configurations that create duplicates strike at the root of unique assignment; severe because a single misconfiguration can duplicate across entire runs.
rows.sat_rat: Range-collision checks exist but cross-run coordination gaps remain a recognized source of latent duplication risk. Allocation configuration is a setup-time activity that rarely gates throughput. Configuring non-overlapping ranges still demands careful operator judgement when multiple runs share a pool. Poorly bounded allocations waste serial ranges but the financial impact is minor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of production runs configured with an incorrect product, package-level, or destination-market identifier
rows.imp_rat: Runs configured with incorrect product/level/market identifier corrupts the entire batch's identity; severe because whole-run misidentification is a major compliance failure.
rows.sat_rat: Configuration validations help, but incorrect identifier selection at changeover remains a leading real-world error. Identifier selection is a setup activity, not a runtime bottleneck. Correct product/level/market selection at setup is error-prone and skill-dependent. A misconfigured run can scrap all its output, a major cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of rejected, destroyed, or recalled units retaining an active serial status
rows.imp_rat: Rejected/destroyed/recalled units retaining active serial status leaves live identities for illegitimate product; severe because active serials on removed product invite diversion and false authentication.
rows.sat_rat: Decommission workflows exist but destroyed units retaining active status remains a recurring reconciliation gap. Disposition status updates post at disposition time. Operators must trigger status changes for rejected units, a diligence risk. Active status on destroyed units risks compliance exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the run configuration conflicts with the approved product, market, or packaging order
rows.imp_rat: Run configuration conflicting with approved product/market/order misdirects the whole run; severe because a single config error can render an entire batch non-compliant.
rows.sat_rat: MES/serialization recipes and electronic batch records reduce configuration errors, but manual parameter selection still permits mismatches that verification may not catch. Run configuration checks happen at setup and can delay start if conflicts surface late. Correct configuration relies on the setup operator matching product, market, and order parameters, a judgement-heavy step. A misconfigured run wastes an entire batch, so errors here are expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of packages whose serial identifier is linked to incorrect product, lot, or expiration data
rows.imp_rat: Serial linked to incorrect product/lot/expiration data corrupts the data behind the identifier; severe because wrong expiry/lot data reaching market is a safety and compliance risk.
rows.sat_rat: Serialization systems bind serials to lot/expiry from batch records, but master-data entry errors still propagate incorrect associations that are hard to detect inline. Master-data linkage is applied automatically at commissioning without adding run time. Correct linkage depends on accurate master-data setup, which requires careful data-management discipline. Wrong lot/expiry linkage can force recalls, a very high-cost failure versus low control cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that prior-run data remains active in the new run context
rows.imp_rat: Prior-run data remaining active in a new run risks applying wrong identity/config; severe because residual data can misidentify an entire new batch.
rows.sat_rat: Formal line-clearance and session-reset procedures are standard practice, but manual clearance and lingering buffers occasionally leave prior-run data active. Clearing prior-run data adds a brief changeover step but rarely a major delay. Line-clearance procedures guide operators, reducing dependence on individual memory. Residual data causing a mixup can invalidate a batch, making the failure costly though the control itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of packaged units that proceed downstream without identifier verification
rows.imp_rat: Packaged units proceeding without verification skips the verify step of the job; severe because defective codes escape detection and reach patients.
rows.sat_rat: Mature serialization lines enforce that every unit passes a verification station, but bypass modes, sensor gaps, and manual reintroduction of units create recurring escape paths. Line control systems flag unverified units without adding meaningful delay to the run. Interlocks that halt the line when verification is bypassed reduce reliance on operator vigilance. The verification-gating logic is bundled into existing line software, imposing little extra cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of saleable units advanced without successful completion of required serialization verification
rows.imp_rat: Units advanced without successful required verification skips a core CFJ step; severe because unverified saleable units enter distribution.
rows.sat_rat: Line-level verification gating is mature and usually blocks unverified units, though manual overrides and offline handling create occasional escapes. Verification steps are inline on packaging lines so they add little incremental time. Vision/verification stations operate largely automatically, needing limited operator skill. Rework of unverified units carries scrap and handling cost when they slip through.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of activating serialization parameters for the wrong batch, product, or destination market
rows.imp_rat: Activating serialization parameters for the wrong batch/product/market misconfigures identity centrally; severe as the entire run may be wrongly serialized.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management with approvals reduces but does not eliminate the risk of activating parameters for the wrong batch or market. Parameter activation is a setup step adding little runtime. Selecting the correct batch/market profile is an error-prone human judgement at setup. Wrong-market activation can render an entire run non-compliant and unsellable.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of labeled units absent from the commissioned population
rows.imp_rat: Labeled units absent from the commissioned population means physical units lack a valid record; severe because uncommissioned units fail downstream authentication.
rows.sat_rat: Batch reconciliation reports expose extra labeled units, but resolving the root cause still requires manual work. Reconciliation runs at batch close, not blocking production. Investigating unaccounted labeled units demands analyst effort. Reconciliation discrepancies drive costly manual investigation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that nonconforming serialized labels are accepted under the approved verification criteria
rows.imp_rat: Nonconforming labels accepted under approved criteria means the verify function is flawed at its acceptance rule; severe because systematically bad product passes as good.
rows.sat_rat: Grade-based acceptance is standard, but poorly calibrated or overly lenient criteria still let nonconforming labels pass, making this a recurring residual gap. Acceptance criteria are applied in real time by the verification system, so no added time is incurred. The risk hinges on how well QA engineers defined the acceptance criteria, which demands specialist grading judgement that is not always consistent. Setting and validating grading thresholds consumes engineering effort but little ongoing material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of labels left without an assigned serial during the production run
rows.imp_rat: Labels left without assigned serials during the run directly fails the assign step; severe because unserialized labels represent non-compliant product.
rows.sat_rat: Inline verification reliably catches labels without serials, keeping this failure rare. Missing-serial detection is inline and immediate. Little operator skill is needed since the system flags unserialized labels. Unserialized labels cause rejects consuming material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serial identifiers attributed to the wrong run, lot, or product
rows.imp_rat: Serials attributed to the wrong run/lot/product corrupts identity attribution central to traceability; severe because genealogy errors undermine recalls and reporting.
rows.sat_rat: Run context binding is generally solid but changeover and manual overrides still cause occasional misattribution. Attribution is automatic at event capture with no added time. Correct run/lot binding depends on setup accuracy rather than skill in-line. Misattribution triggers costly reconciliation and possible batch rejection.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an unreadable or malformed identifier is recorded as passing verification
rows.imp_rat: Recording an unreadable/malformed identifier as passing corrupts the verify outcome; severe because unscannable codes reach market and fail downstream authentication.
rows.sat_rat: ISO/IEC grade thresholds catch most unreadable codes, but marginal-grade codes and decoder tolerance still occasionally get recorded as passing. Vision systems grade readability instantly during the run with no time penalty. Distinguishing a malformed code that still decodes requires careful threshold setup that depends on scarce vision-system expertise. Grading logic is built into existing scanners, so incremental cost is low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of serialization exceptions remaining unresolved when a sterile fill-finish batch reaches disposition
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved exceptions at sterile fill-finish disposition is high-stakes; severe because sterile product with open serialization issues released risks patient harm and major recalls.
rows.sat_rat: Exception tracking exists but ensuring zero open serialization exceptions at sterile disposition is inconsistently enforced, leaving a real gap. Clearing exceptions before sterile batch disposition can extend release timelines under pressure. Judging exception closure for sterile product demands experienced QA oversight. Held sterile batches carry high inventory and expiry cost while exceptions clear.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that discrepancies among serial assignment, label printing, verification, and audit records remain undetected during the assessment
rows.imp_rat: Undetected discrepancies among assignment/print/verify/audit records means integrity failures escape assessment; severe because unresolved mismatches undermine release confidence.
rows.sat_rat: No unified reconciliation view exists in most environments, so undetected discrepancies across the four record sets are a significant, chronic risk. Detecting cross-record discrepancies during assessment is slow and largely manual. Correlating assignment, print, verification, and audit records demands scarce senior analyst expertise. Manual four-way reconciliation is expensive in expert time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serial identifiers associated with the wrong product or lot during production
rows.imp_rat: Serials tied to the wrong product/lot during production misidentifies units; severe because incorrect product-lot association can trigger recalls and market rejection.
rows.sat_rat: Once set correctly the context holds, but changeover residue and mid-run edits can still misassociate serials to the wrong product or lot. Context is bound at run start so no runtime time is added. Relies on correct setup and changeover discipline by staff. Wrong lot association during production risks batch-level rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of printed serial identifiers differing from their assigned values
rows.imp_rat: Printed serials differing from assigned values breaks the link between assign and print; severe because the physical code no longer matches the record, defeating traceability.
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop print verification comparing the read code to the assigned value catches most print discrepancies, though print-quality drift still causes some. Print-and-verify happens inline at speed so comparison adds no delay. Automated readback compares printed to assigned value without operator interpretation. Mismatched prints become scrap, but immediate detection limits the quantity wasted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of previously assigned, commissioned, retired, or reserved serials marked as available
rows.imp_rat: Previously used/retired/reserved serials marked available reintroduces non-unique numbers into assignment; severe because it directly risks duplicate assignment central to the CFJ.
rows.sat_rat: Status enforcement prevents most improper availability, though bulk imports can occasionally mis-set status. Availability status is set transactionally without delay. Correct status modeling requires configuration expertise. Wrongly available serials risk duplicate-use costs downstream.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of packages passing the serialization station without verified identifiers
rows.imp_rat: Packages passing the station without verified identifiers bypasses a core verification control; consequences are serious as unverified product enters distribution.
rows.sat_rat: Standard camera verification tied to a reject mechanism catches unverified packages consistently, though marginal reads still slip occasionally. Inline verification cameras confirm each package at line speed with no operator delay. Automated verify-and-reject logic requires minimal operator judgement to enforce. Unverified escapes risk costly recalls, but reject gates limit waste to the individual bad unit.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of current market serialization requirements omitted from readiness criteria
rows.imp_rat: Current market requirements omitted from readiness criteria means the system is not validated against actual rules; severe because non-compliant product may pass readiness.
rows.sat_rat: Readiness checklists exist but are maintained manually and lag fast-changing global mandates, so omissions recur. Building readiness criteria is a pre-run preparatory activity, not a run-time delay. It relies on scarce regulatory affairs expertise to enumerate current per-market requirements. Omitted requirements surface as costly compliance failures rather than direct control cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of verified units whose printed codes differ from assigned data
rows.imp_rat: Verified units whose printed codes differ from assigned data means verification passed a mismatch; severe because the verify control failed on identity-critical data.
rows.sat_rat: Print-and-verify with inline vision is a mature standard that catches most printed-vs-assigned mismatches reliably. Inline verification cameras compare printed to assigned data in real time without slowing throughput. Automated vision comparison removes the need for manual read-back skill. Mismatch detection prevents costly downstream recalls, a strong cost benefit.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of labels whose printed serial identifier differs from the identifier assigned to the saleable unit
rows.imp_rat: Printed serial differing from the assigned identifier breaks the assign-to-print integrity central to the job; severe because the label misrepresents the unit's identity.
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop print-then-verify is a mature, widely deployed control that makes printed-vs-assigned mismatch uncommon. Print-and-verify occurs at line speed so timing is not the issue. Automated print-verify closed loops remove most operator dependence. Mislabeled units become scrap, but the automated loop keeps this waste low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serial identifier records lost or corrupted during production
rows.imp_rat: Lost or corrupted serial records destroys the traceability data underpinning the job; severe because missing records can force holds and breach data-integrity requirements.
rows.sat_rat: Buffering and databases make record loss uncommon, though communication drops can still corrupt or lose events. Data capture is automatic and does not add line time. Recovery of lost records needs IT skill when it happens. Lost records can force re-runs or batch investigations at high cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of packaged units whose verification result is associated with a different serial identifier
rows.imp_rat: Verification result tied to a different serial invalidates the verify record's linkage; severe because pass/fail evidence is attributed to the wrong unit.
rows.sat_rat: Tight scanner-to-record timing on modern lines makes result-to-serial mismatches uncommon, though high-speed line desyncs occasionally cause them. Verification results bind to serials instantly during scanning with no delay. Correct association is handled by the line software, requiring little operator skill once configured. Mismatched results are cheap to prevent within existing systems but costly if they trigger wrong dispositions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of labeled units whose verification event is absent from the serialization records used for product release
rows.imp_rat: Missing verification events in release records break the evidentiary chain for the verify step; severe because release cannot demonstrate compliance and units may be held.
rows.sat_rat: Most verification events are reliably persisted, but network drops, buffering, or manual stations occasionally leave gaps in the release dataset. Verification events are captured in near real time so latency is minimal. Event logging is automatic, requiring little skill to ensure capture. Missing verification records force costly investigations before release.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of rejected, scrapped, or destroyed units whose serials remain commissioned
rows.imp_rat: Scrapped/destroyed units whose serials remain commissioned leaves active identities for nonexistent product; severe because it invites diversion and authentication of counterfeits.
rows.sat_rat: Decommission events are supported but scrapped units retaining commissioned status is a common reconciliation gap. Status updates for scrapped units occur at disposition time. Requires operator action to decommission destroyed units, prone to omission. Stale active serials on destroyed units risk downstream compliance costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that integrity certification proceeds while unresolved serialization exceptions remain
rows.imp_rat: Certification proceeding with unresolved exceptions weakens the release gate; severe because unverified issues escape into released product, though it is a governance control.
rows.sat_rat: Systems flag open exceptions but enforcement that certification cannot proceed while any remain is often procedural rather than hard-gated, leaving a real recurring gap. Exception reviews before certification add review time but are typically scheduled into the release workflow. Judging whether an exception is truly resolved still leans heavily on experienced QA reviewers. Manual exception reconciliation before certification consumes meaningful QA labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of sterile fill-finish batches whose records cannot reconstruct identifier assignment, label printing, and verification activities
rows.imp_rat: Sterile batches whose records cannot reconstruct assignment/print/verify defeats audit reconstruction for high-risk product; severe because it blocks defensible release of sterile lots.
rows.sat_rat: Serialization records usually support reconstruction, but stitching assignment, print, and verification into one defensible narrative for sterile batches is imperfect. Reconstructing full activity history for a batch is time-consuming across systems. Assembling a coherent reconstruction requires skilled investigators familiar with each data source. Reconstruction effort is expensive when records are dispersed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of unreadable applied identifiers remaining undetected during production
rows.imp_rat: Unreadable applied identifiers undetected in production let defective codes escape the verify net; severe because they only surface at downstream scanning failures.
rows.sat_rat: Some unreadable applied codes still pass undetected when grading thresholds are lenient or coverage is imperfect. Inline detection avoids downstream time loss when it works. Depends on properly configured grading rather than operator eyes. Undetected unreadable codes escape and risk costly rejection later in the chain.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of units assigned a serial identifier outside the authorized allocation
rows.imp_rat: Serials outside authorized allocation break the regulatory-authorized numbering scheme central to serialization; severe as unauthorized serials can be rejected by authorities.
rows.sat_rat: Systems dispensing only from an authorized, downloaded allocation pool rarely emit out-of-range codes. Allocation ranges are loaded before the run so no per-unit time is spent checking bounds. Software restricts dispensing to the loaded range, needing no operator arithmetic. Out-of-range serials risk regulatory rejection, but pre-loaded pools prevent the associated waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a printed code being compared with assigned data for the wrong unit
rows.imp_rat: Comparing a printed code against the wrong unit's data invalidates the verification logic; severe because good/bad decisions are made on mismatched references.
rows.sat_rat: Encoder-based unit tracking usually keeps comparisons unit-aligned, but jam recovery and reject-station timing can still cross wires. Verification is synchronized to unit position so alignment adds no delay when tracking works. Correct camera-to-unit tracking is configured by engineers, limiting operator burden. Misattributed verification can pass bad units, incurring downstream cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of commissioned serial identifiers shipped with a status prohibited by the destination market
rows.imp_rat: Commissioned serials shipped with a prohibited status breaches destination-market rules; severe because prohibited-status product can be rejected or seized at import.
rows.sat_rat: Repository status gates catch many cases, but destination-market status enforcement varies by system maturity and configuration, leaving meaningful exposure. Market-status checks at shipment are automated and fast where configured. Correctly configuring market-specific status rules demands specialized regulatory knowledge. Blocked or recalled shipments from wrong-status exports are very costly when they occur.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serial identifiers with conflicting status across serialization and batch-release records
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting serial status across serialization and batch-release records creates release ambiguity; severe because contradictory statuses can allow non-compliant release.
rows.sat_rat: Serialization and ERP/batch systems are often loosely integrated, so status conflicts between them are a recurring, incompletely controlled problem. Reconciling serialization and batch-release records adds time at disposition. Interpreting conflicting statuses across two systems requires cross-domain expertise. Manual cross-system reconciliation is labor-intensive per batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the integration architecture permitting duplicate or invalid serial identifiers during assignment
rows.imp_rat: An architecture permitting duplicate/invalid serials at assignment is a systemic uniqueness flaw; severe because it can generate widespread duplicates central to the CFJ.
rows.sat_rat: Established serialization systems enforce unique-number allocation reliably, making duplicates rare where deployed. Uniqueness checks run in real time within mature serialization platforms with negligible delay. Vendor number-management modules handle uniqueness so little specialist judgement is needed. Duplicate identifiers cause expensive recalls, but standard number pools largely prevent them cheaply.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of provisioned serials associated with the wrong product, package level, market, or production order
rows.imp_rat: Provisioned serials tied to wrong product/level/market/order misroutes identity upstream; severe because it can cascade into mislabeled market shipments.
rows.sat_rat: Order-to-serial binding is systematized but human selection errors at provisioning still occur despite validations. Association is defined once per order, not per unit, so time is not the issue. Master-data selection demands attentive setup and cross-checking by the operator. Wrong-order association can invalidate a whole run's provisioned serials at significant cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of rejection resolutions leaving serial identifiers in incorrect lifecycle statuses
rows.imp_rat: Rejection resolutions leaving incorrect lifecycle statuses corrupts serial state; severe because wrong statuses can wrongly release rejected units.
rows.sat_rat: Rejection resolution frequently leaves serials in incorrect lifecycle states, a persistent weakness. Fixing lifecycle statuses after rejection resolution is manual and time-consuming. Operators must apply correct lifecycle transitions, a subtle and error-prone task. Incorrect statuses can leave serials unusable or wrongly commissioned, wasting them.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of packaging hierarchies containing incorrect parent-child serial associations
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect parent-child associations break aggregation hierarchies central to track-and-trace; severe because broken aggregation fails distribution scanning and reporting.
rows.sat_rat: Automated aggregation is standard but notoriously error-prone from missed scans, manual repacks, and partial cases, leaving frequent hierarchy inaccuracies. Aggregation is captured in real time during packing without added delay. Reliable aggregation depends on correct scanner/packing-station setup and disciplined handling, requiring attention. Broken parent-child links force re-aggregation or manual rework, a notable labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of printed serial identifiers that are unreadable during verification
rows.imp_rat: Printed identifiers unreadable during verification undermine the verify step; severe as unreadable codes cannot be authenticated later.
rows.sat_rat: Inline print inspection catches unreadable codes reliably, but print-quality drift from heads, dust, and substrate variation keeps unreadable rates persistently nonzero. Unreadable prints are caught inline instantly, but the resulting rejects and reprints consume run time. Maintaining print quality demands ongoing operator attention to printers, ribbons, and substrates. Poor print quality wastes labels, materials, and serial numbers, a recurring material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serialized label data elements that conflict with approved artwork or market rules
rows.imp_rat: Label data conflicting with approved artwork/market rules makes printed labels non-compliant; severe because non-conforming labels can be rejected at market entry.
rows.sat_rat: Artwork management systems help, but manual reconciliation of serialized data fields against artwork remains error-prone and inconsistently controlled. Artwork/data conflicts are ideally caught at proofing, not during the run, so no run delay if done. Reconciling label data against approved artwork and market rules is expert, detail-intensive work. Conflicts discovered late waste printed labels and force reprints, a real material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of provisioned serials duplicating another serial intended for assignment
rows.imp_rat: Provisioned serials duplicating others intended for assignment seeds duplicates before assignment; severe because it undermines uniqueness at source.
rows.sat_rat: Provisioning engines validate against issued sets so duplicate-provision defects are uncommon in mature deployments. Provisioning is fast and duplicate screening adds little delay. Uniqueness validation is automated, reducing reliance on operator vigilance. Rejecting duplicate provisions costs almost nothing in materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of labels whose encoded serial differs from the human-readable serial
rows.imp_rat: Encoded serial differing from human-readable serial creates conflicting identities on one label; severe because scanners and inspectors read different values, failing authentication.
rows.sat_rat: Single-source template generation makes encoded/human-readable divergence rare when templates are validated. Encoded and human-readable fields print together with no added time. Template-driven printing removes manual transcription, lowering skill demand. Mismatched fields cause label scrap, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of identifiers eligible for assignment that are duplicate, invalid, or previously used
rows.imp_rat: Duplicate/invalid/used serials in the eligible pool poisons assignment at its source; severe because it propagates non-unique identifiers into the run.
rows.sat_rat: Number-management systems track used/available status reliably, though cross-system pool reuse and manual imports occasionally introduce invalid or reused identifiers. Pre-validated number pools are provisioned ahead of runs, so no run-time delay results. Pool validation is automated by the serialization system, minimizing operator judgement. Managing valid pools is inherent to the system with negligible marginal cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serialization events associated with the wrong product, lot, or run
rows.imp_rat: Serialization events tied to the wrong product/lot/run corrupts event data central to track-and-trace; severe because it distorts reported genealogy.
rows.sat_rat: Automated event tagging is mostly reliable but shares the same changeover-residue risks affecting association accuracy. Event tagging happens automatically without operator time. Relies on correct run configuration, not runtime skill. Wrong-run events force reconciliation labor and holds.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of serial codes unreadable at production line speed
rows.imp_rat: Codes unreadable at line speed frustrate downstream scanning central to track-and-trace; severe because they cause aggregation failures and market rejections.
rows.sat_rat: Print quality at high speed drifts with ink, substrate and web tension, so unreadable codes remain a recurring issue despite grading tools. Reading occurs at line speed but poor codes can slow throughput via rejects. Achieving readable codes at speed needs print-quality tuning expertise. Unreadable codes become scrap and consume relabeling effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serial identifiers with unresolved lifecycle statuses at run closure
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved lifecycle statuses at run closure leaves indeterminate serial states; severe because ambiguous statuses can allow improper release or block valid units.
rows.sat_rat: Unresolved lifecycle statuses at closure remain a recurring reconciliation burden. Clearing unresolved statuses at closure extends closeout time. Requires analysts to interpret and correct lifecycle states. Unresolved statuses delay release and hold inventory value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serial identifiers whose recorded statuses conflict with physical label dispositions
rows.imp_rat: Recorded statuses conflicting with physical label dispositions breaks record-to-reality alignment; severe because status mismatches can wrongly release or hold units.
rows.sat_rat: Status-versus-physical conflicts are a chronic reconciliation pain, especially around rejects and rework. Reconciling status vs physical disposition is a manual, time-consuming review. Requires analyst skill to match electronic statuses to physical label handling. Discrepancies trigger investigations and can hold batch release.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of resolved units whose physical serial markings conflict with electronic event records
rows.imp_rat: Resolved units whose physical markings conflict with electronic records break correspondence after rework; severe because unresolved conflicts can release mismatched product.
rows.sat_rat: Rejected/resolved units are a known hotspot where physical markings and event records still diverge. Resolving physical-vs-electronic conflicts on resolved units is manual and slow. Requires careful investigator judgement to reconcile markings and events. Unresolved conflicts risk compliance findings and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of restored serials carrying the wrong lifecycle status
rows.imp_rat: Restored serials carrying the wrong lifecycle status misrepresents serial state after recovery; severe because incorrect statuses can wrongly enable or block units.
rows.sat_rat: Restore paths after interruption are less well controlled, so wrong-status restoration recurs. Restore operations complete quickly when invoked. Restoring correct lifecycle status after recovery needs skilled intervention. Wrong restored status causes reconciliation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of labels printed with unreadable or incomplete codes after restart
rows.imp_rat: Labels printed unreadable/incomplete after restart produces defective codes at a vulnerable moment; severe because restart defects can escape if verification is disrupted.
rows.sat_rat: Restart often produces a burst of poor prints that verification catches but does not prevent. Post-restart reverification adds line time. Restart print-quality checks depend on operator attention. Unreadable post-restart labels waste materials and serials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serial identifiers with conflicting statuses across line and track-and-trace records
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting statuses across line and track-and-trace records creates reconciliation ambiguity; severe because it can allow wrongly-statused units to release.
rows.sat_rat: Line-to-T&T status conflicts remain common due to timing and integration gaps between systems. Cross-system reconciliation between line and T&T adds closure time. Resolving status conflicts needs cross-system knowledge. Conflicts delay reporting to regulators and hold product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required printed identifier elements omitted from verification scope
rows.imp_rat: Required identifier elements omitted from verification scope means the verify check is incomplete; severe because unchecked fields can carry defects to market.
rows.sat_rat: Verification recipes typically cover core identifier elements, but supplementary printed elements are inconsistently included in scope, leaving recurring gaps. Verification scope is defined at setup and does not add run time once set. Defining which printed elements to verify requires QA judgement that is easy to under-specify. An omitted element from scope is cheap operationally but risks costly downstream rejections.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of finished-unit serial identifiers missing from the completed run record
rows.imp_rat: Finished-unit serials missing from the completed run record leaves gaps in the traceability record; severe because incomplete records impede release and audit.
rows.sat_rat: Reconciliation reports catch most missing serials, but late or dropped events still leave occasional gaps at closure. Completeness is checked at closure, adding reconciliation time. Reconciling missing serials needs analyst attention. Missing records delay batch release, tying up inventory value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of applied codes that cannot be decoded during verification
rows.imp_rat: Applied codes that cannot be decoded during verification undermine the verify step; severe because undecodable codes cannot be authenticated in the field.
rows.sat_rat: Verification readers reliably decode most codes when well set up, but marginal print quality causes recurring decode failures. Decoding happens inline in real time. Camera and lighting setup to ensure decodability demands technician skill. Undecodable codes waste labeled units and serials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of label fields mapped to the wrong encoded or human-readable value
rows.imp_rat: Label fields mapped to the wrong encoded/human-readable value corrupts printed identity data; severe because mislabeled fields can misroute or misidentify product.
rows.sat_rat: Validated label templates catch most mapping errors, but new-template errors still surface before qualification. Field mapping is a template-design activity, not a runtime delay. Correct field-to-value mapping requires skilled template configuration and review. Mismapped fields can invalidate an entire label design run at real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of assigned serial identifiers with unresolved disposition at the product-release decision
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved disposition at release leaves serial state ambiguous at the decision gate; severe because releasing with undetermined status risks shipping non-compliant units.
rows.sat_rat: Systems report status but reliably driving every assigned identifier to a defined disposition by release is an inconsistent, gap-prone practice. Resolving open dispositions before the release decision extends release timelines. Determining correct disposition for stranded identifiers requires experienced serialization/QA judgement. Chasing unresolved dispositions ties up review labor per lot.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unauthorized changes to approved serialization master data before or during a production run
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized changes to serialization master data compromise data integrity governing assignment; severe because tampered master data can corrupt a whole run and breach data-integrity rules.
rows.sat_rat: Role-based access and change control are mature, though mid-run master-data locking is not universally airtight. Change controls add procedural time but not runtime delay. Enforcing master-data change control depends on disciplined role-based access. Unauthorized master-data changes can spoil runs at high cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of status events applied to a serial other than the intended serial
rows.imp_rat: Status events applied to the wrong serial corrupt lifecycle state central to track-and-trace; severe because incorrect statuses can wrongly enable or block units.
rows.sat_rat: Event-to-serial routing is systematized but manual status corrections still occasionally hit the wrong serial. Status events post in near-real-time. Automated event routing reduces manual matching effort. Misapplied status events cause reconciliation labor rather than material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting a partially obscured identifier as readable
rows.imp_rat: Accepting a partially obscured identifier as readable is a verify leniency error; moderately severe because degraded codes may fail later downstream scans.
rows.sat_rat: Verification cameras sometimes accept partially decodable codes, so partial obscuration slipping through remains a real gap. Grading at speed adds no operator time but marginal reads can stall decisions. Setting grade thresholds to reject partial obscuration needs vision-grading knowledge. Accepting an obscured code that later fails causes downstream rework costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of configured date or lot formats that violate destination-market rules
rows.imp_rat: Date/lot formats violating market rules make labels non-compliant; severe because the destination may reject shipments over format non-conformance.
rows.sat_rat: Market-rule templates exist but keeping them current across many jurisdictions leaves recurring compliance gaps. Format configuration is done once per market at setup. Knowing each market's date/lot rules requires specialized regulatory knowledge. Non-compliant formats can trigger rejected shipments at high cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required serial-assignment, label-printing, or verification records are unavailable when the assessment begins
rows.imp_rat: Required records unavailable when assessment begins blocks verification of compliance; severe because missing records can force holds and fail audit.
rows.sat_rat: Records are generally retained and available, but fragmentation across line, site, and repository systems occasionally leaves records missing at assessment time. Retrieving records at assessment start can be slow when data is spread across systems. Knowing where and how to pull each record type requires system-specific expertise. Record retrieval effort adds preparation cost before each assessment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of units reaching verification with a required code field missing
rows.imp_rat: Units reaching verification with a missing required code field means the print step failed; severe because incomplete identifiers cannot satisfy serialization requirements.
rows.sat_rat: Field-presence verification is standard and catches missing required codes consistently. Missing-field detection happens at verification without delay. Automated presence checks reduce reliance on operator inspection. Units failing for missing fields are rejected, wasting product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of commissioned serials lacking a corresponding labeled unit
rows.imp_rat: Commissioned serials lacking a corresponding labeled unit creates phantom records; severe because it breaks reconciliation and can mask missing or diverted product.
rows.sat_rat: Reconciliation flags orphan serials reliably, though closing them out remains a manual burden. Detected at batch reconciliation rather than in real time. Chasing commissioned serials without units needs skilled reconciliation. Investigation labor is the main cost of these discrepancies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serial identifiers lacking a valid link to the applicable release lot
rows.imp_rat: Serials lacking a valid link to the release lot breaks lot traceability; severe because unlinked serials cannot be reconciled at release or during recalls.
rows.sat_rat: Commissioning normally binds identifiers to a lot, but reprints, exceptions, and manual interventions still produce identifiers with weak or missing lot links. Lot-linking is applied at commissioning so timing is not the concern. Association is automated, needing little operator skill when configured. Broken lot links trigger costly traceability investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of applicable serialization obligations translated incorrectly into integration criteria
rows.imp_rat: Obligations translated incorrectly into integration criteria is an upstream design-quality gap; moderately severe as errors propagate but are caught before production if reviewed.
rows.sat_rat: Without standardized mappings, interpretation varies between people and mistranslations slip through unnoticed. Translating obligations into integration criteria is iterative and often revisited late, adding delay. Correct translation hinges on rare individuals who bridge regulatory language and technical architecture, making errors common. Misinterpretation drives expensive redesign but the interpretive step is cheap in materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of applicable serialization obligations lacking traceable evidence in the architecture evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Obligations lacking traceable evidence in the architecture evaluation is an audit-completeness gap; moderately severe because it weakens defensibility rather than producing direct defects.
rows.sat_rat: Traceability matrices are inconsistently maintained, so many obligations lack durable evidence links. Assembling traceable evidence for each obligation is a laborious documentation task typically deferred. Building defensible traceability requires disciplined systems-engineering practice that many teams lack. Evidence gaps trigger audit findings and rework that cost far more than maintaining traces would.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required serialization entries missing at run closure
rows.imp_rat: Required entries missing at run closure leaves incomplete records; severe because closure gaps block release and undermine audit defensibility.
rows.sat_rat: Closure checklists flag missing entries reliably but resolving them remains a manual, sometimes incomplete effort. Missing entries surface at run closure, extending closeout time. Resolving missing entries requires knowledgeable review. Incomplete records block release and hold inventory.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that serialization records are altered without authorized and traceable approval
rows.imp_rat: Serialization records altered without traceable approval is a data-integrity breach; severe because unauthorized alterations can invalidate the entire audit trail and compliance standing.
rows.sat_rat: Validated, access-controlled serialization systems make unauthorized untraceable changes rare where GxP controls are enforced. Approval workflows for record changes add controlled delay but are routine. Validated systems handle authorization enforcement without special user skill. Access controls are already provisioned so marginal cost is low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable serialization obligations omitted from the architecture evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Obligations omitted from architecture evaluation is a design-completeness gap; moderately severe because missed requirements may surface later but are one step removed from production.
rows.sat_rat: Obligation checklists exist but are frequently incomplete for edge markets, so omissions are a recurring and consequential failure. Compiling the full obligation set across markets is a slow manual research effort that regulatory-intelligence teams rarely complete promptly. Identifying every applicable obligation demands scarce regulatory-affairs and serialization expertise that few evaluators possess in full. Missing an obligation surfaces later as costly rework or remediation, but the upfront research cost itself is modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required serialization fields missing or invalid in the batch setup
rows.imp_rat: Missing/invalid serialization fields in batch setup misconfigures the run at the start; severe because setup errors can corrupt the entire batch's serialization.
rows.sat_rat: Setup validation checks catch most missing or invalid fields before the run starts. Pre-run verification adds setup time but prevents runtime loss. Operators must enter batch fields correctly, an error-prone manual step. Missing fields cause aborted setups and lost time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a required serialization data connection is unavailable before run authorization
rows.imp_rat: A required data connection unavailable before run authorization delays or blocks proper setup; moderately severe as it prevents start but is caught before production.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-run connectivity checks are common but network, EPCIS, and provider outages still intermittently leave required connections unavailable before authorization. An unavailable data connection at authorization directly stalls run start, costing time. Diagnosing connectivity issues requires IT/integration skill not always on the floor. Stoppages waiting for connections are costly in idle line time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between parameter values used for identifier printing and verification
rows.imp_rat: Mismatched printing vs verification parameters can cause valid codes to fail or bad codes to pass; severe because it undermines the reliability of the verify decision.
rows.sat_rat: Integrated systems sharing one recipe largely prevent print/verify mismatch, though loosely coupled setups still drift. Shared parameter sets avoid re-entry time between print and verify. Ensuring both stations use the same recipe needs integration setup skill. Parameter mismatch causes false rejects and wasted units.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of provisioned serial records lost or corrupted before assignment
rows.imp_rat: Provisioned records lost/corrupted before assignment threatens the source data for the job; severe because corrupted pools can produce invalid or duplicate assignments.
rows.sat_rat: Database persistence and backups make pre-assignment serial loss rare in validated systems. Loss is detected after the fact, adding recovery time. Recovering corrupted serial records needs specialized IT skill. Lost provisioned serials waste allocated ranges and investigation effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of retired serials returned to active status
rows.imp_rat: Retired serials returned to active status can reintroduce duplicate or invalid identifiers; severe because reactivated serials may authenticate as legitimate wrongly.
rows.sat_rat: Lifecycle state machines generally block retired-to-active transitions, keeping this rare. Status reactivation is a quick transaction when it occurs. Preventing retired-serial reactivation depends on status-model enforcement. Reactivated retired serials risk compliance problems more than direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unapproved serialization parameter changes being active at run start
rows.imp_rat: Unapproved parameter changes active at run start misconfigure serialization from the outset; severe because the entire run inherits the unapproved settings.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic change control and locked recipes make unapproved active changes uncommon in validated environments. Change control is a pre-run gate, not a runtime cost. Approval workflows offload the judgement from the operator to a controlled process. Unapproved changes can invalidate output at significant cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of provisioned serials violating required format or allocation rules
rows.imp_rat: Provisioned serials violating format/allocation rules introduce non-compliant identifiers; severe because they can be rejected at reporting or market entry.
rows.sat_rat: Format/allocation-rule validation is a standard provisioning feature that catches most violations. Format validation runs instantly during provisioning. Rule configuration needs expertise but enforcement is automated thereafter. Format violations waste provisioned serials, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of doubting that every saleable unit has a valid unique identifier at certification
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about unique validity at certification reflects assurance quality rather than a direct defect; moderately severe since low confidence delays certification but is not itself a shipped error.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unexplained gaps in the serial identifier sequence during production
rows.imp_rat: Unexplained gaps in the serial sequence signal reconciliation problems; moderately severe as they trigger investigation but do not directly ship defects.
rows.sat_rat: Sequence tracking flags gaps reliably, but explaining them still requires manual reconciliation and some remain unresolved. Gap detection is automated post-run, not adding line time. Investigating gaps needs analyst skill to reconcile events. Unexplained gaps trigger investigation labor and potential batch holds.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serials provisioned without approved authorization
rows.imp_rat: Serials provisioned without approved authorization introduces unsanctioned numbers; severe because unauthorized serials may be rejected by regulators or fail authentication.
rows.sat_rat: Approval-gated provisioning is standard in validated systems, making unauthorized provisioning uncommon. Authorization checks are near-instant once workflows are configured. Electronic approval gating reduces reliance on operator judgement. Unauthorized provisioning is cheap to prevent with existing controls.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of exhausting the provisioned serial pool before all batch units receive serials
rows.imp_rat: Exhausting the serial pool before all units are serialized stops assignment; moderately severe as it halts production but is generally caught before shipping unserialized units.
rows.sat_rat: Low-pool alerts exist, but under-provisioning still occasionally exhausts pools before batch completion. Pool depletion is monitored continuously without added delay. Planners must size pools correctly, needing some forecasting skill. Exhaustion mid-run halts the line, a costly stoppage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the configured serial allocation is exhausted before planned run output is completed
rows.imp_rat: Allocation exhausted before planned output halts assignment; moderately severe as it stops the run but is typically resolved without shipping defects.
rows.sat_rat: Allocation thresholds are tracked but occasional undersizing against planned output still occurs. Allocation monitoring is real-time. Sizing allocation to run output requires planning judgement. Allocation exhaustion stops production at significant cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of provisioned serials inaccessible to the assignment workflow when requested
rows.imp_rat: Provisioned serials inaccessible to the workflow when requested stalls assignment; moderately severe as it disrupts flow but is usually recoverable before shipment.
rows.sat_rat: Serial access is usually reliable, but integration or network hiccups occasionally make requested serials unavailable. Inaccessible serials cause a hard wait, so timeliness is directly impacted. Diagnosing access failures requires IT/integration skill. Blocked assignment idles the line, driving cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of available serials being exhausted during a production run
rows.imp_rat: Serials exhausted during a run stops assignment; moderately severe as it causes stoppages but is usually caught before non-compliant shipment.
rows.sat_rat: Buffered pools and alerts make mid-run exhaustion uncommon but not eliminated when top-up is slow. Serial availability is monitored live during the run. Requesting timely top-ups needs operator awareness. Running out mid-run causes expensive downtime.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of exhausting available serials before the production run is complete
rows.imp_rat: Exhausting serials before run completion halts assignment; moderately severe as it stops the run but rarely ships defective product directly.
rows.sat_rat: This near-duplicate depletion concern is served by the same mature threshold-alert practice with occasional gaps. Depletion tracking is continuous. Minimal skill needed once threshold alerts are configured. Exhaustion before completion incurs costly line stops.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of planned run demand not covered by valid serial identifiers
rows.imp_rat: Run demand not covered by valid serials threatens completion of assignment; moderately severe as it causes stoppages but is typically caught before shipment.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-run pool provisioning against planned quantities is standard, but demand changes and overage allowances still leave occasional coverage gaps mid-run. Provisioning shortfalls can halt a run, so this can directly cost run time when pools run dry. Estimating demand and requesting adequate pools requires planning judgement that is often manual. Under-provisioning triggers costly stoppages while over-provisioning wastes ranges, so balancing has real cost implications.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of authorized serial identifiers becoming unavailable before batch completion
rows.imp_rat: Authorized serials becoming unavailable before batch completion halts assignment; moderately severe as it stops the line but is usually recoverable without shipping defects.
rows.sat_rat: Provisioning large serial pools before batch start makes exhaustion uncommon, though connectivity to central systems can still interrupt supply. Pre-loaded serial pools mean availability rarely delays the run. Ordering adequate serials ahead requires planning but little in-line skill. Running short mid-batch can waste line time and materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of serialization record changes lacking an attributable audit trail
rows.imp_rat: Record changes lacking an attributable audit trail is a data-integrity governance gap; severe because missing attribution can invalidate records during audit, though not a direct product defect.
rows.sat_rat: Part -style audit trails are mature and comprehensive, so changes lacking an attributable trail are very uncommon in validated systems. Audit-trail capture is automatic and imposes no added time. No operator skill is needed since trails are system-generated. Audit logging is a built-in feature with negligible incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serials commissioned before the corresponding label code is printed and verified
rows.imp_rat: Serials commissioned before their code is printed/verified is a sequencing error; moderately severe because it can create prematurely active serials but is often reconcilable.
rows.sat_rat: Commission-after-verify sequencing is configurable and generally well controlled in mature lines. Ordering of commission vs print is enforced inline in real time. Sequence enforcement is automated, reducing operator dependence. Premature commissioning risks reconciliation waste of little material value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of line stoppages caused by serial identifiers becoming unavailable for assignment
rows.imp_rat: Line stoppages from unavailable serials is a throughput/delay issue peripheral to the compliance CFJ; low severity because it delays rather than ships defects.
rows.sat_rat: Buffered local serial pools make such stoppages infrequent but not eliminated when upstream systems fail. When serials do run out, restoring supply causes a measurable line stoppage. Recovery often needs IT or supervisor intervention rather than the operator alone. Stoppage time is lost productive capacity and labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of readable identifiers classified as unreadable during production
rows.imp_rat: Readable identifiers classified as unreadable is a false-reject verification error; low severity because it causes scrap and delay rather than shipping bad codes.
rows.sat_rat: False-unreadable classification persists as a tuning-dependent nuisance at line speed. Over-classification as unreadable causes reject-review time. Reducing false unreadables needs vision-tuning expertise. Readable units scrapped as unreadable waste product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to make assigned serial identifiers available for printing
rows.imp_rat: Time to make serials available for printing is a throughput/latency concern peripheral to compliance; low severity because it delays rather than causes defective output.
rows.sat_rat: Local buffering reliably keeps serials ready, with rare exceptions when provisioning fails. Pre-provisioned and cached serial pools make codes available for printing almost instantly. No operator skill is needed once pools are loaded. Any delay here idles the line, but caching largely avoids that cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of valid serial codes rejected at production line speed
rows.imp_rat: Valid codes rejected at line speed is a false-reject yield/throughput issue peripheral to compliance; low severity because good product is delayed, not shipped bad.
rows.sat_rat: False-reject rates on valid codes remain a persistent nuisance at line speed, tied to lighting and focus variability. False rejects trigger stoppages and manual review that cost line time. Tuning camera thresholds to avoid over-rejection requires vision-system expertise. Good units wrongly rejected are wasted product and packaging.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of serialized labels rejected because their printed codes are unreadable
rows.imp_rat: Labels rejected because printed codes are unreadable is largely a scrap/yield outcome; low severity since the reject correctly prevents bad product from advancing.
rows.sat_rat: Grade-checking cameras catch unreadable codes, but print-quality drift still generates ongoing reject rates. Unreadable rejects are caught inline without slowing the line materially. Print-quality tuning requires operator skill to set up correctly. Rejected labels consume serials and materials, a recurring cost driver.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of valid codes incorrectly rejected during verification
rows.imp_rat: Valid codes incorrectly rejected during verification is a false-reject yield concern peripheral to compliance; low severity because good product is scrapped, not shipped bad.
rows.sat_rat: Modern grading reduces false rejects, but threshold sensitivity still causes conforming units to be rejected periodically. False-reject decisions are made inline without delay. Tuning grading thresholds to avoid false rejects requires expertise. False rejects scrap good units and serials, a direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of conforming units rejected during code verification
rows.imp_rat: Conforming units rejected during verification is a false-reject/yield loss peripheral to the compliance job; low severity as it protects rather than harms downstream.
rows.sat_rat: Vision verification is mature but over-rejection of good units remains a recurring line-yield concern. Verification disposition is immediate. Minimizing good-unit rejection needs skilled camera calibration. Rejecting conforming units wastes product and serials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of prior-batch serialization settings remaining active after changeover
rows.imp_rat: Prior-batch settings left active after changeover risks wrong-market serials with moderate severity but secondary relevance; capped by ranking.
