rows.sat_rat: Print-verify loops reliably catch payload divergence where standard vision is installed. Payload verification is inline and immediate. Print-and-inspect systems compare printed to assigned automatically with minimal skill. Mismatched print payloads cause scrap, but inline checks limit the cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a unit advancing without a valid serial-identifier verification result
rows.imp_rat: A unit advancing without valid verification defeats the verify job with meaningful severity, but is capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Line-integrated verify-and-reject is mature, so units advancing without verification are uncommon where deployed. Inline reject mechanisms act in real time to halt unverified units. Reject-station automation removes reliance on operator vigilance. Bad units advancing cause expensive recalls, but automated rejects keep prevention affordable.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of serialization events recorded outside the required sequence or with conflicting lifecycle states across systems
rows.imp_rat: Out-of-sequence or conflicting lifecycle states harm cross-system consistency with moderate severity, held under 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Distributed timing means conflicting states occur regularly and are not fully prevented today. Cross-system sequence and state conflicts are often detected only after the fact. Diagnosing lifecycle-state ordering needs deep event-model expertise. Out-of-sequence events cause data corrections that are labor-intensive and costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serial identifiers assigned to more than one saleable unit
rows.imp_rat: Serials assigned to more than one saleable unit break uniqueness at the core, yet the ceiling constrains this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Serialization platforms reliably guarantee one identifier per unit when correctly implemented. Uniqueness enforcement is instantaneous within serialization allocation logic. Number-pool management handles single-assignment automatically with little expert input. Duplicate saleable-unit assignment is costly if it escapes, but prevention is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serial identifiers altered or truncated during transfer between enterprise and line systems
rows.imp_rat: Serials altered or truncated in transfer corrupt identifier integrity with moderate severity, but the batch cap applies.
rows.sat_rat: Field-length and transfer transformations still cause truncation failures that are only partly caught. Truncation issues are usually found late during integration testing, not upfront. Spotting field-length and truncation risks needs careful interface engineering skill. Altered identifiers cause data-integrity remediation costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of assigned identifier records omitted from handoffs between enterprise and line systems
rows.imp_rat: Assigned records omitted from handoffs risk missing serials with moderate severity, but capped by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Handoff completeness is imperfect, so missing records remain a recurring issue. Detecting omitted handoff records typically requires reconciliation runs after the fact. Ensuring complete handoffs needs solid integration and reconciliation expertise. Dropped records force costly investigations and reconciliation effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of encoded package identifiers differing from their assignment records
rows.imp_rat: Encoded package identifiers differing from assignment records undermine print integrity with moderate severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Code verification against assignment records is a mature, reliable inline practice. Encoded-code verification runs inline without delay. Barcode grading tools automate encode checking, needing little expertise. Encoding mismatches cause rework, but inline grading keeps it inexpensive to catch.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of invalid serial identifiers accepted as valid during verification
rows.imp_rat: Accepting invalid serials as valid directly defeats the verify function with real severity, but the batch ordering caps it.
rows.sat_rat: Standard code-grading and verification tools reliably reject invalid identifiers where properly configured. Verification decisions occur inline at speed with negligible added time. Vision and verification systems automate the check, requiring little operator judgement. Accepting invalid identifiers risks recalls, but mature verifiers keep this cheap to prevent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of verification outcomes linked to the wrong serial identifier or package
rows.imp_rat: Verification outcomes linked to the wrong serial corrupt the verify record with moderate severity, capped by the batch.
rows.sat_rat: Tight camera-to-record coupling makes wrong-identifier linkage uncommon in mature setups. Verification-to-identifier linkage happens inline with no time penalty. Automated association reduces the need for manual matching skill. Mislinked outcomes cause costly investigation but are relatively infrequent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unauthorized changes to identifier assignments, lifecycle states, or package relationships remaining undetected
rows.imp_rat: Undetected unauthorized changes to assignments/states touch record integrity with real severity, but the batch cap applies.
rows.sat_rat: Audit trails exist but active detection of unauthorized changes is inconsistently implemented. Detecting unauthorized changes often relies on periodic audits rather than real-time alerts. Setting up tamper detection needs security and audit expertise. Undetected unauthorized changes risk costly compliance breaches.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of reconciliation adjustments lacking traceable rationale or authorization
rows.imp_rat: Reconciliation adjustments lacking rationale are an administrative traceability gap of modest relevance, capped by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Reconciliation adjustments without traceable rationale still occur when documentation discipline lapses. Documenting adjustment rationale adds reconciliation time. Requires disciplined operator/analyst documentation. Untraceable adjustments cause audit findings and rework.

rows.sat_rat: Backup and retention policies make audit history loss before retention expiry rare in validated systems. Retention protection operates continuously in the background. Retention management is automated, needing little skill. Long-term storage carries modest cost.

Investigation labor is the main cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of verification events lacking complete code, comparison, or disposition evidence
rows.imp_rat: Verification events lacking complete evidence weaken defensibility with modest relevance and moderate severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Verification stations log code, comparison, and disposition, though evidence completeness varies with configuration. Verification evidence is captured inline in real time. Automatic evidence capture reduces operator burden. Evidence storage adds modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of uncertainty about whether code exceptions are resolved before release

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of disclosing a serialization record to an unauthorized or incorrect recipient

Recipient routing is configured once, needing limited ongoing skill. Improper disclosure risks compliance cost, but prevention is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of duplicate or conflicting events transmitted for the same serial

Open deviations drive rework and investigation cost that programs must absorb.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of serialization risk ratings relying on incomplete or unverified evidence

rows.sat_rat: Risk ratings are often assigned on incomplete or unverified evidence under time pressure, undermining confidence. rows.stmt: Reduce the number of inspector follow-up questions that lack defensible answers about the serialization approach

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt that traceability, auditability, or electronic-record integrity gaps remain unrecognized before sign-off

rows.sat_rat: Definition mismatches between enterprise and line systems are common and only partly reconciled. Harmonizing event definitions across systems is a lengthy alignment exercise. rows.imp_rat: Conflicting interpretations of obligations has moderate severity but limited direct relevance to the job, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Divergent interpretations of obligations recur and are seldom fully reconciled. Reconciling regulatory, quality, and technical interpretations consumes lengthy alignment cycles. Bridging these interpretive communities requires rare cross-disciplinary expertise. Interpretation conflicts drive expensive rework and disputes.

rows.imp_rat: Disposition scenarios lacking compliant handling is a coverage gap of modest relevance and moderate severity, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Disposition handling for edge cases is frequently incomplete in current criteria. Defining compliant dispositions for all reject/rework cases is slow scenario work. Compliant disposition design requires quality and regulatory expertise. Non-compliant dispositions cause scrap accounting errors and audit exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of required serialization records being unavailable for regulatory inquiry or trading-partner exchange
rows.imp_rat: Records unavailable for regulatory inquiry has compliance severity but modest direct relevance to the core job, held under 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Compliant serialization repositories reliably keep records available for inquiry and exchange. Retrieving serialization records for inquiry is generally fast within compliant repositories. Standard reporting interfaces require limited specialist skill to query. Regulated retention is a manageable ongoing cost with mature systems.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of incompatible serial-identifier state and event definitions across enterprise and line systems
rows.imp_rat: Incompatible state/event definitions across systems is an integration ambiguity of modest relevance and moderate severity.
rows.sat_rat: State/event incompatibilities recur where standards differ between layers. Aligning state and event models across systems takes extended coordination time. State-model reconciliation demands scarce cross-domain modeling skill. Incompatible state definitions drive costly translation logic and errors.

rows.imp_rat: Late discovery of undocumented cross-system dependencies is a design-phase risk of limited direct job relevance, held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Dependency discovery is incomplete in typical practice, so surprises remain common. Undocumented dependencies surface late, causing schedule shocks after recommendation. Surfacing hidden dependencies demands seasoned architects who know system internals. Late discovery forces expensive redesign and re-validation.

Conflicting lifecycle states can persist for extended periods before reconciliation resolves them. Shortening conflict windows requires skilled synchronization design. Prolonged state conflicts tie up investigation effort and hold inventory.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of package hierarchy relationships differing between enterprise and line records
rows.imp_rat: Package hierarchy differences between records pose modest relevance and moderate aggregation severity, held under 2.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of uncertainty that an enterprise-to-line interface or validation risk remains unsurfaced before the selection decision
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty an interface/validation risk remains unsurfaced has moderate severity but modest relevance, capped.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of transaction timing and state incompatibilities left undiagnosed between identifier assignment, printing, and verification
rows.imp_rat: Undiagnosed timing/state incompatibilities are an integration concern of modest relevance and moderate severity, held under 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Timing-related integration faults are hard to detect and commonly slip through analysis. Timing and state incompatibilities often go undiagnosed until runtime problems appear. rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of interoperability gaps emerging only at expected production volumes and line speeds
rows.imp_rat: Interoperability gaps at production volume are a scaling risk of modest direct relevance and moderate severity, held at the ceiling.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of conflict among interface specifications, deployed configurations, and observed serial-identifier exchanges
rows.imp_rat: Conflict among specs, configs, and observed exchanges is an integration ambiguity of modest relevance and moderate severity.

rows.imp_rat: Integration criteria lacking measurable acceptance conditions is an upstream ambiguity with limited direct relevance and modest severity, held under 2.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that capital approval is denied because serialization noncompliance and sterile fill-finish disruption consequences are unsubstantiated
rows.imp_rat: Capital denial from unsubstantiated consequences has moderate severity but peripheral relevance to the core job, held under 2.0.

Quantifying noncompliance and fill-finish disruption consequences demands specialized regulatory and financial-risk expertise rarely resident in one person. Weak substantiation risks losing the entire capital allocation, an outsized cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a qualified supplier cannot deliver the required serialization capability by the compliance deadline
rows.imp_rat: Supplier unable to deliver by deadline has compliance severity but peripheral relevance to the core job, capped.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier qualification and delivery track records exist but late or failed deliveries against serialization deadlines remain a common, painful surprise. Compliance deadlines are fixed and supplier lead times often consume most of the available window, leaving little slack. Judging supplier delivery capacity against a regulatory deadline requires procurement and technical due-diligence skill that is unevenly applied. Late delivery risk is usually not priced into the case, so overruns land unexpectedly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that capital authorization occurs too late to complete serialization readiness before the applicable regulatory deadline
rows.imp_rat: Late capital authorization risking deadline has compliance severity but peripheral relevance to the core job, capped.

Regulatory serialization deadlines are fixed and widely publicized, so procurement typically has clear lead-time targets, but capital approval cycles are still notoriously slow and prone to last-minute crunches. Judging how early authorization must occur to leave room for validation demands cross-functional program expertise that is often thin in a purchasing function. Late authorization forces expedited procurement and overtime, inflating cost, though the pain is felt more as schedule risk than as budget waste.

Ensuring sterile products actually meet serialization rules post-deployment requires deep regulatory and technical validation expertise beyond procurement. Residual noncompliance triggers costly holds and remediation, but the executor cannot cost-control their way out of a technical failure.

Securing funding in the correct budget window requires financial-planning coordination that purchase executors rarely control alone. The core risk is that money simply isn't available when required, a resource-availability failure that established capital-planning processes only partially prevent.
